EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several cars were stuck on dirt roads in Randolph county due to flooding.

A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Moist flow behind the line in the afternoon was strong enough to initiate isolated thunderstorms which became strong to severe. Localized gusty winds and hail up to 1 was reported. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell on powerlines at intersection of Bradbury and Lowry Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a 4-6 hour period during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 25th, a large-scale MCS/damaging wind event moved nearly due south, slamming much of north and central Georgia, including the Atlanta metro area. More than 170 storm reports, mainly wind damage were documented across the 96-county forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Limbs broken and shingles blown off of a roof between Old Grassdale Rd NW and Peoples Valley Rd NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough moving through the Ohio Valley coincided with peak diurnal heating, ample atmospheric moisture, minor shear, and moderate downdraft potential. Scattered thunderstorms developed across Northwest Georgia and the Atlanta Metro, causing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a power line on Strickland Pasture Rd and Wilson Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a tree down at Hogansville Rd near Hogansville Pump Station Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms moved into Northwest GA and continued southeast through the late afternoon and evening hours. Storms were strongest over parts of far northwest GA, and generally weakened as they moved to the southeast. Primary hazards included damaging winds with several reports of large hail as mid level lapse rates were rather steep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Paris Mountain Rd near Crawford Lake Cir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall in the Big Bend region of Florida as a category 3 hurricane. As Idalia moved inland to the northeast, it brought tropical storm-force winds and heavy rain to much of south Georgia, including far southeast counties of NWS Peachtree City CWFA (Telfair to Toombs county) where winds gusted between 40-50 mph and 3 to 6 inches of rain occurred. Damage to trees and powerlines was observed in Emanuel, Montgomery, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, and Wheeler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported 5 trees down between Summertown and Midville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary located draped across west-central and northwest GA on the 28th and 29th. Significant tropical moisture ahead of Hurricane Idalia led to several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe. The main hazards included damaging winds and torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported flooding at Dorsett Shoals Road and Kings Highway. Radar estimates indicate that between 3 and 4 inches of rain occurred over the area, resulting in the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough pushed through the Ohio Valley bringing strong lift and shear into Northern Georgia. Significant instability and northwest flow made conditions ideal for Mesoscale Convective Complex development. Storms developed substantial severe winds over large areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees on houses and a large tree down on the road in the Belvedere Park area along Glenwood Rd as well as San Gabriel Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave riding along a frontal boundary produced heavy rainfall over north Georgia on the morning of July 10. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were common over northwest Georgia, with portions of Floyd county receiving above 4 inches. Although convective activity diminished into the pre-dawn hours as this system moved southward into Atlanta, it was not before flash flooding was reported in the city of Rome.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported flooding along Calhoun Road, north of Model High School, and of Ward Mountain Road. Both of the roadways were flooded due to high water from Dozier Creek. Radar estimates indicate that between 3.5 and 4.5 inches occurred over the area, resulting in high runoff rates and quickly rising creeks and tributaries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple pine trees were blown down on Highway 313 just north of Highway 32.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of flooded roadways in Lee county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast, southward moving MCS produced widespread wind damage during the afternoon hours of June 30th, along the Alabama and Georgia state line. Widespread winds of 50-70 mph were either reported or estimated based on the damage, mainly to trees and powerlines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a 4-6 hour period during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 25th, a large-scale MCS/damaging wind event moved nearly due south, slamming much of north and central Georgia, including the Atlanta metro area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of tree down on a vehicle along Neely Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Moist flow behind the line in the afternoon was strong enough to initiate isolated thunderstorms which became strong to severe. Localized gusty winds and hail up to 1 was reported. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 inch hail via social media near the intersection of Steve Tate highway and Cove  Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary located draped across west-central and northwest GA on the 28th and 29th. Significant tropical moisture ahead of Hurricane Idalia led to several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe. The main hazards included damaging winds and torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down across River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong to marginally severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms developed along I-20, east of Atlanta. Storms caused localized wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down across Highway 22 near the Hancock county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on roadway at Oak Hills St and Eastern Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on power line near the intersection of Jodeco Way and Noahs Ark Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at Lily Pond Road and Newton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a 4-6 hour period during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 25th, a large-scale MCS/damaging wind event moved nearly due south, slamming much of north and central Georgia, including the Atlanta metro area. More than 170 storm reports, mainly wind damage were documented across the 96-county forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported power lines and trees down on the 1600 block of Highway 411.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Moist flow behind the line in the afternoon was strong enough to initiate isolated thunderstorms which became strong to severe. Localized gusty winds and hail up to 1 was reported. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down near intersection of Little Texas Valley Rd NW and Mountain Rd NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary located draped across west-central and northwest GA on the 28th and 29th. Significant tropical moisture ahead of Hurricane Idalia led to several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe. The main hazards included damaging winds and torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down along GA Hwy 166 and West Chapel Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall in the Big Bend region of Florida as a category 3 hurricane. As Idalia moved inland to the northeast, it brought tropical storm-force winds and heavy rain to much of south Georgia, including far southeast counties of NWS Peachtree City CWFA (Telfair to Toombs county) where winds gusted between 40-50 mph and 3 to 6 inches of rain occurred. Damage to trees and powerlines was observed in Emanuel, Montgomery, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, and Wheeler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a tree downed along SR 46 near Soperton due to Tropical Storm Idalia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on I-16 eb between mile marker 69 and mile marker 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on home along Gratis Rd near Bradley Gin Rd.

Significant instability and northwest flow made conditions ideal for Mesoscale Convective Complex development. Storms developed substantial severe winds over large areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance moved out of Alabama and into North and Central Georgia along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. Overperforming instability and minor shear allowed thunderstorms to become strong to severe, particularly in the Atlanta Metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a tree down across roadway near intersection of H D Atha Rd and Jersey Social Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of thunderstorms in Tennessee moved into North Georgia in the early afternoon hours of the 3rd. Thunderstorms began to redevelop into a Mesoscale Convective Complex (MCS) developing severe winds across a broad area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported a tree down on powerlines at the intersection of Nebo Road and Swan Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall in the Big Bend region of Florida as a category 3 hurricane. As Idalia moved inland to the northeast, it brought tropical storm-force winds and heavy rain to much of south Georgia, including far southeast counties of NWS Peachtree City CWFA (Telfair to Toombs county) where winds gusted between 40-50 mph and 3 to 6 inches of rain occurred. Damage to trees and powerlines was observed in Emanuel, Montgomery, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, and Wheeler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported trees and powerlines down along GA Hwy 135 between Higgston and Uvalde. Hardest hit areas were south of Hwy 280.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of a tree fell on a person resulting in minor injuries near the 300 block of Gaines Oak Way. Additional trees reported down on Suwanee Dam Road, nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported flooding at Pearl Nix Parkway at Dorsey, Jesse Jewel Parkway at Atlanta Highway, Davis Street and Summit Street. Radar estimates indicate that between 1.5 and 3 inches of rain occurred over the area, resulting in the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across much of north and central GA during the afternoon and evening hours of July 21st. Numerous reports of damaging winds, along with significant flash flooding (around downtown Atlanta) occurred along the I-20 corridor. Another area impacted by damaging winds occurred from Stewart County (far SE part of the FFC forecast area) to near Swainsboro in Emanuel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Employee reported flooding near the Fox Theater. Radar estimates indicate that between 3 and 4 inches of rain occurred over the area, resulting in the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent northwest flow aloft coupled with a moist, unstable environment allowed for multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms to develop each afternoon. The storms moved generally from north to south across the area, with much of the wind damage occurring in the afternoon/evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of powerline down on 600 block of Henderson Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of a large tree down on a car along Barrentt Shoals Rd near Forest .

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed withing a large scale straight line wind|event which occurred across Pickens County. Similar to the other|tornado in Pickens county, this tornado developed on the north|side of a large surge in winds as denoted both on radar and|widespread wind damage on the ground. This EF0 tornado (peak winds estimated around 80 mph) first developed in a wooded area northwest of Carver Mill Road to the|west of the intersection of Carver Mill Rd and Dean Trail. Trees|were snapped or uprooted along its path which nearly paralleled |Carver Mill Rd until it crossed Hwy 53. Trees and powerlines were |downed along Carver Mill Rd, Dean Mill Rd, Red Branch Rd, Bradley |Rd and Perrow Hwy. The tornado tracked southwest crossing Hwy 53 |near Thompson Lane where trees were snapped and uprooted. Some |minor roof damage was also noted in the area. The tornado |dissipated shortly after crossing Hwy 53.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three transformers were blown near the Publix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to Scattered diurnally driven thunderstorms formed over Central GA in the afternoon and evening hours. Storms moved generally Eastward with a few becoming severe mostly just South of Macon. Gusty winds of up to 60mph brought down several trees in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast, southward moving MCS produced widespread wind damage during the afternoon hours of June 30th, along the Alabama and Georgia state line. The damage (in west-central GA) occurred from Polk County south to around the Columbus area (Muscogee County). Widespread winds of 50-70 mph were either reported or estimated based on the damage, mainly to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported two trees with powerlines down along Lovic Davis Rd; one near the intersection with Indian Bend Dr and the other near the intersection with Garrett Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary located draped across west-central and northwest GA on the 28th and 29th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerline down on the 400 block of Turkey Creek Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 4 trees down on roadways in central and northern Banks county per EM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This particular tornado developed shortly after a large scale|straight line wind event unfolded across Pickens County. It should|be noted that while the tornado did considerable damage in the |span in which it was on the ground, the large scale straight line |wind damage across Pickens county southeastward was far more |considerable. The tornado formed on the north side a surge in the |winds along Long Swamp Church road very near the Bent Tree Lodge |and Vineyard and Angel on Horseback pavilion. Several trees were |snapped and uprooted in this area. The tornado continued eastward |through a wooded area crossing Dank road behind several homes on |the north side of Fitts Road. The tornado reached maximum |intensity as it crossed Fitts and Dank Roads where numerous trees |were snapped and uprooted, 1 home lost its roof, another home |sustained major roof damage and an outbuilding was destroyed. The |tornado continued southeast snapping and uprooting trees before |dissipating as it reached Skyline Drive. Peak winds were estimated around 95 mph (EF-1 tornado).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Moist flow behind the line in the afternoon was strong enough to initiate isolated thunderstorms which became strong to severe. Localized gusty winds and hail up to 1 was reported. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 2 inch hail via social media in Moreland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms moved into Northwest GA and continued southeast through the late afternoon and evening hours. Storms were strongest over parts of far northwest GA, and generally weakened as they moved to the southeast. Primary hazards included damaging winds with several reports of large hail as mid level lapse rates were rather steep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees and powerlines down on roads and houses in the Winterville  area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: House fire due to lightning strike. Structure was a total loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto power lines on Cardinal Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at Radium Springs Road and Putney Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary located draped across west-central and northwest GA on the 28th and 29th. Significant tropical moisture ahead of Hurricane Idalia led to several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe. The main hazards included damaging winds and torrential rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance moved out of Alabama and into North and Central Georgia along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. Overperforming instability and minor shear allowed thunderstorms to become strong to severe, particularly in the Atlanta Metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KATL measured a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down along Wolf Creek Rd near Oak Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down within the city of Loganville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough pushed through the Ohio Valley bringing strong lift and shear into Northern Georgia. Significant instability and northwest flow made conditions ideal for Mesoscale Convective Complex development. Storms developed substantial severe winds over large areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a tree down on the 400 block of Scott Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall in the Big Bend region of Florida as a category 3 hurricane. As Idalia moved inland to the northeast, it brought tropical storm-force winds and heavy rain to much of south Georgia, including far southeast counties of NWS Peachtree City CWFA (Telfair to Toombs county) where winds gusted between 40-50 mph and 3 to 6 inches of rain occurred. Damage to trees and powerlines was observed in Emanuel, Montgomery, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, and Wheeler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported that Telfair County firefighter was injured when a tree came down. The person is said to be in stable condition at a regional hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down on a structure along Cedar Shoals Dr near Spring Creek Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees and power lines were blown down on Railroad Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed into the afternoon and evening as a frontal zone settled southward across SE GA and an impulse moved across the front. Significant heat and surface moisture in addition to steepening lapse rates ahead of the mid-level impulse created volatile surface-based instability which supported severe downbursts/outflows (50-70 mph) and hail. Given the near alignment of steering flow and the initiating boundary, the potential for waves or training convection created a flood threat as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alma, Bacon County Airport, GA ASOS measured a gust of 51 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a 4-6 hour period during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 25th, a large-scale MCS/damaging wind event moved nearly due south, slamming much of north and central Georgia, including the Atlanta metro area. More than 170 storm reports, mainly wind damage were documented across the 96-county forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down along us Hwy 319 and Oaky Grove Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall in the Big Bend region of Florida as a category 3 hurricane. As Idalia moved inland to the northeast, it brought tropical storm-force winds and heavy rain to much of south Georgia, including far southeast counties of NWS Peachtree City CWFA (Telfair to Toombs county) where winds gusted between 40-50 mph and 3 to 6 inches of rain occurred. Damage to trees and powerlines was observed in Emanuel, Montgomery, Telfair, Toombs, Treutlen, and Wheeler counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple trees and powerlines down around the city of Glenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a stationary boundary located draped across west-central and northwest GA on the 28th and 29th. Significant tropical moisture ahead of Hurricane Idalia led to several thunderstorms becoming strong to severe. The main hazards included damaging winds and torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported flooding near the intersection of Fairplay Road and Mt Vernon Road due to high water on Big Sandy Creek. Radar estimates indicate that between 6 and 7 inches of rain occurred over the northern portion of Morgan county, resulting in the flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported flooding along Beechnut Lane and around homes. The flooding was due to highwater on Rose Creek and nearby ponds. Radar estimates indicate that between 5 and 6 inches of rain occurred over the area, resulting in the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of tree down on Oak Valley Rd near Woodridge LP.

Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Jim Ross Rd near the intersection with Fair Haven Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large squall-line (Mesoscale Convective System) moved across mainly North and East-Central GA during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 20th. Conditions were very favorable for damaging winds along and ahead of well-developed cold pool, with high instability and elevated Downdraft-CAPE. Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey of northern Cherokee County was done to assess whether|or not a tornado occurred.  The NWS assessment concluded that|widespread significant wind damage occurred and that a tornado|path within the large scale wind damage was not discernible.  The|particular area of focus started along Fincher Road east along|Dixon Road, crossing Upper Burris Road south to Land Road. |Significant tree damage and pockets of more significant tree|damage was noted in and around Dixon Road east to Upper Burris|Road.  Roof damage to homes in the area was also noted and used|to help estimate overall max wind speeds.  Tree fall patterns|along Dixon Road east across Upper Burris south to Land Road were|suggestive of a downburst in the region with a fanning out|pattern observed on on the ground. It should be noted that damage|was observed across an area over 3.5 miles wide.  Tree damage|extended north along Upper Bethany road over 1.5 miles north of|Land Road.  The survey concluded that maximum wind speeds within|the more significant pockets of damage likely reached 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent northwest flow aloft coupled with a moist, unstable environment allowed for multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms to develop each afternoon. The storms moved generally from north to south across the area, with much of the wind damage occurring in the afternoon/evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down along with roof damage on hwy 115 between Rhine and Telfair county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave out of Texas moved across the Southeast along a stationary front centered on Central GA. Thunderstorms ahead of and along the line developed severe winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a tree down on a house along Lakeshore Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance moved out of Alabama and into North and Central Georgia along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. Overperforming instability and minor shear allowed thunderstorms to become strong to severe, particularly in the Atlanta Metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported a tree down along Connell Ave SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms moved into Northwest GA and continued southeast through the late afternoon and evening hours. Storms were strongest over parts of far northwest GA, and generally weakened as they moved to the southeast. Primary hazards included damaging winds with several reports of large hail as mid level lapse rates were rather steep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down at Moons Grove Church Rd and Johnnie Chandler Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In a 4-6 hour period during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 25th, a large-scale MCS/damaging wind event moved nearly due south, slamming much of north and central Georgia, including the Atlanta metro area. More than 170 storm reports, mainly wind damage were documented across the 96-county forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Center reported trees down on Highway 5 at Highway 166, Highway 5 at Hollis Road, and powerlines down at Deer Run Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An East-West oriented broken line of storms moved south through the CWA in the afternoon and evening hours. As storms passed over the Atlanta Metro, a few storms became strong with isolated strong winds of up to 60 mph, most likely from wet microbursts. A storm just north of Atlanta managed to produce 1 hail .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 inch hail at Powers Ferry Rd and Delk Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to Scattered diurnally driven thunderstorms formed over Central GA in the afternoon and evening hours. Storms moved generally Eastward with a few becoming severe mostly just South of Macon. Gusty winds of up to 60mph brought down several trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and powerline down across S highway 41 near Alpine Valley dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area in the morning of the 11th. Moist flow behind the line in the afternoon was strong enough to initiate isolated thunderstorms which became strong to severe. Localized gusty winds and hail up to 1 was reported. Another line of thunderstorms moved into the area through the middle of the night and early morning of the 12th, bringing localized damaging wind-gusts to parts of North GA. Storms then redeveloped with afternoon instability in the afternoon across the far SE CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down blocking roadway at intersection of Vinings Court and Paces Vale Place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple vehicles flooded in Albany which required rescue.

Widespread damage occurred from far north Georgia to east-central GA (Warren County), with very intense winds (80-90+mph) estimated from damage across parts of Cherokee and Forsyth counties. A couple of brief  embedded weak tornadoes occurred in Cherokee County from this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approx location of tree down reported by EMA, Satinwood Dr and Overview Ct.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent northwest flow aloft coupled with a moist, unstable environment allowed for multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms to develop each afternoon. The storms moved generally from north to south across the area, with much of the wind damage occurring in the afternoon/evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Friendship Church Rd near Hwy 117.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on the 14th of June, numerous thunderstorms tracked across north and central Georgia. A cluster of severe storms resulted in widespread reports of damaging wind and large hail in a swath from northern Troup county to Henry/Butts County (south of Atlanta). Additional strong to severe storms moved across our far southern counties in central GA during the morning hours of June 15th. Little to no damage was reported in these more rural areas. Later on the 15th, scattered severe storms over far north GA produced localized wind damage and large hail from Whitfield County to White County, and isolated flash flooding in Gainesville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media picture of at least golf ball sized hail in Grantville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across much of north and central GA during the afternoon and evening hours of July 21st. Numerous reports of damaging winds, along with significant flash flooding (around downtown Atlanta) occurred along the I-20 corridor. Another area impacted by damaging winds occurred from Stewart County (far SE part of the FFC forecast area) to near Swainsboro in Emanuel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported several trees down on homes in the Veazy community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across an unstable airmass located mostly over Mississippi, Alabama, and Western Georgia in the afternoon hours. Storms were able to produce outflow strong enough to further develop spotty clustering of thunderstorms across North and Northeast GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of tree down along Grubb St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of thunderstorms in Tennessee moved into North Georgia in the early afternoon hours of the 3rd. Meanwhile, daytime heating across Central Georgia helped to destabilize the moist airmass in place. Thunderstorms began to redevelop into a Mesoscale Convective Complex (MCS) developing severe winds across a broad area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were reported down in the vicinity of Jackson Lake,  including on Jackson Lake Rd, Alcovy N Rd, and County Line Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough moving through the Ohio Valley coincided with peak diurnal heating, ample atmospheric moisture, minor shear, and moderate downdraft potential. Scattered thunderstorms developed across Northwest Georgia and the Atlanta Metro, causing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Old Corinth Rd near Earl North Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance moved out of Alabama and into North and Central Georgia along a nearly stationary frontal boundary. Overperforming instability and minor shear allowed thunderstorms to become strong to severe, particularly in the Atlanta Metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a tree down across roadway at intersection of Gunnel Rd and Due West Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A scattered line of thunderstorms moved into a moderately unstable airmass south of I20. Several storms became strong with damaging winds. A few reports of particularly large hail were also noted in East Central GA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a tree down at Linton Road and 2nd Ave W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough pushed through the Ohio Valley bringing strong lift and shear into Northern Georgia. Significant instability and northwest flow made conditions ideal for Mesoscale Convective Complex development. Storms developed substantial severe winds over large areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on a house along S Van Wert Road near Walker Pond Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The middle part of June was unusually stormy across the area. Numerous rounds of strong to severe storms impacted the region with multiple squall lines that produced severe winds as well as supercells that produced large hail and tornadoes.  Heavy rain also occurred over a multi-day period, which saturated the ground and led to some instances of flash flooding.  ||The synoptic pattern featured well above average 250 mb zonal wind anomalies, which were traceable back to an active subtropical jet stream.  Greater than average instability was also present, in part due to anomalously warm Gulf of Mexico SSTs.  This combination of above average shear from the active subtropical jet and above average instability from the warmer than average SSTs provided a synoptic environment favorable for severe weather as upper level shortwaves moving through the area.  This pattern persisted for many days, leading to an unusually large amount of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down on Pearson Road near Woodcrest Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Idalia made landfall Wednesday morning August 30th around 745 AM EDT along the|Florida Big Bend near Keaton Beach as a category 3, with the inner core moving NE across the|Suwannee River Valley. The inner wind core of Idalia gradually weakened through the morning while,|heavily impacting Suwannee and Hamilton counties in NE Florida and Echols county SE Georgia as the|circulation tracked NE. Widespread tree, power line and structure damage occurred across Suwannee|and Hamilton counties, with estimated winds of 80-100 mph based on damage surveys with isolated|pockets of peak wind gusts near major hurricane strength. Outer rainbands on the eastside of Idalia|impacted all of SE Georgia and NE FL through the day, with early morning tornadoes across coastal SE|Georgia and widespread tropical cyclone wind gusts through the day causing tree damage and power|line damage at least somewhere across NE Florida and SE Georgia. Measured gusts during the peak of|the hurricane acorss the local forecast area were 60-80 mph. Flooding rainfall impacted locations in|the direct path of the weakening inner core across the Suwannee River Valley and southeast Georgia,|especially from eastern Hamilton county toward Baxley and Jesup GA. The peak rainfall amounts were|up to 5-8 inches. At least 3 tornadoes occurred in southeast GA as rainbands swept northwestward|ahead of the hurricane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical cyclone winds impacted the county on 8/30. Most noted tropical storm force wind damage occurred|NNE to ESE of Nahunta between Highway 301 and Waynesville during the late morning into the evening. An|NWS Storm Survey reported some trees were blown down along Post Road in the NE portion of the county|due to tropical storm force winds. Hardwood trees were snapped and uprooted along Highway 82 east of|Nahunta as well as along adjoining side roads Knox Road and Pembroke Road. Tree damage extended north|of Nahunta along Laurel Road where several small hardwood trees were snapped and larger trees were|uprooted. A local mentioned a tree had fallen onto a powerline in the area.|At 1150, a mesonet site at the EMA office in Hortense measured a wind gust of 40 mph. At 1245, a mesonet|site in Nahunta measured a wind gust of 40 mph. At 1400, the EMA office in Hoboken measured a wind gust|of 51 mph. The EM reported that Hoboken Elementary school suffered significant roof damage. At 1445, the|mesonet site in Nahunta measured a wind gust of 42 mph. At 1450, a mesonet site measured a wind gust of|55 in Hoboken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front stalled across the local forecast area and served as a focus for localized heavy rainfall in diurnally enhanced sea breeze convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front stalled across the local forecast area and served as a focus for localized heavy rainfall in diurnally enhanced sea breeze convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station measured 4.16 inches of rainfall in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal hazard potential increased through the week as high astronomical spring tides coincided with a persistent onshore flow pattern. Coastal hazards include increasingly strong and frequent rip currents, beach erosion and minor to moderate tidal flooding along the coast, within the Intracoastal Waterway and St. Johns River basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On 9/27 at 8:30 pm, the NOS gage at Kings Bay Pier recorded a peak water level of 2.46 ft MHHW datum. Moderate flooding begins at this location at 2.5 ft MHHW. On 9/28 at 9:30 pm, the gage reached 2.39 ft MHHW datum. ||On 9/27 at 9:30 pm, a USGS gage at Cumberland Island Sea Camp Dock measured a peak tide of 2.13 ft MHHW. Moderate flooding begins at 2.2 ft MHHW at this location. On 9/28 at 9:30 pm, the gage reached 2.05 ft MHHW datum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system moved through the area with ample instability and shear for severe weather. Numerous reports of severe weather were received across the tri-state area, although no tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Loch Laurel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Gulf of Mexico and tracked northeast across the Florida Peninsula in the early morning hours of December 17th, then strengthened across warm Atlantic waters while passing along the Southeast Coast, bringing minor impacts in the form of coastal flooding and strong winds across southeast Georgia.||Moderate coastal flooding occurred along the southeast Georgia coast, with the Fort Pulaski Tide Gage (FPKG1) peaking at 10.07 ft MLLW on December 17th, which is the 15th highest tide on record at this location. A daily record rainfall amount of  3.16 inches was also set at the Savannah, GA (KSAV) climate site. Elsewhere, rainfall amounts generally fell within the 2 to 4 inch range across southeast Georgia.||During rapid intensification, the low pressure system displayed central pressures lowering from 1010 mb at 7 AM December 16th to 988 mb at 7 PM December 17th, a 22 mb drop in a 36-hr period. This led to a strong pressure gradient across the region as well as December minimum sea-level pressure records being set at several coastal sites along the Southeast Coast, including at Savannah, GA (KSAV). Observed wind gusts along the coast were generally between 40 and 50 mph, which led to a few trees down across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A McIntosh County 911 Call Center reported a tree down and blocking the road near the intersection of Highway 99 and Ridgeway Road.

A daily record rainfall amount of  3.16 inches was also set at the Savannah, GA (KSAV) climate site. Elsewhere, rainfall amounts generally fell within the 2 to 4 inch range across southeast Georgia.||During rapid intensification, the low pressure system displayed central pressures lowering from 1010 mb at 7 AM December 16th to 988 mb at 7 PM December 17th, a 22 mb drop in a 36-hr period. This led to a strong pressure gradient across the region as well as December minimum sea-level pressure records being set at several coastal sites along the Southeast Coast, including at Savannah, GA (KSAV). Observed wind gusts along the coast were generally between 40 and 50 mph, which led to a few trees down across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was relayed of a large tree down and blocking a trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Respectable instability and moderate lift from a digging upper-level trough initiated scattered thunderstorms across portions of North and Central GA. A couple of storms in the Northwest ATL Metro were able to become strong with damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half Dollar-sized hail at intersection of Brookstone Dr NW and Mclain Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering boundary combined with an approaching shortwave to provide enough support and instability to support numerous showers and thunderstorms, including a few strong to severe storms. A severe thunderstorm over downtown Atlanta produced extremely high rainfall rates, accumulating 1 to 1.25 inches in 15 minutes. In total, this area received 2.5 to 3.0 inches, but in an hour or less, resulting in significant flash flooding of Atlanta landmarks and residential areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and several powerlines down Southwest of the community of Bankhead between Martin Luther King Jr Drive Southwest and US278.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening frontal system moved southeast into an unstable airmass over Central GA. Steep lapse rates and instability meant scattered thunderstorms over Central and East-Central GA developed damaging winds and some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of Golf Ball sized hail on Hwy 358 near Woodland Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers persisted along the wedge boundary on the morning of September 15th. Training showers produced 3 to 4 inch rainfall totals across eastern Putnam, Hancock, Washington and Glascock counties. In Glascock county, runoff from these rainfall amounts resulted in the quick rise of Joes Creek, resulting in a road washout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported Snider Road, between Gibson and Mitchell, was washed out due to highwater on a local creek. Radar estimates indicate that between 2.5 and 3 inches of rain occurred over the area in under an hour, resulting in the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper-level disturbance initiated scattered shower and thunderstorms across North and North-Central GA during the early afternoon hours. Minor instability meant a few thunderstorms became strong to severe. Storms were slow moving and reached the eastern portion of the CWA late in the evening. Late storm consolidation led to isolated severe wind and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and powerlines down at Hwy 98 and Hwy 441.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager relayed more than 40 reports of water rescues, dozens of stalled vehicles, and several flooded buildings and student dorms at Clark Atlanta University. Vehicles parked near Northside Drive and Martin Luther King Jr. Drive were washed into a parking lot near Mercedes-Benz Stadium. Cars could be seen in piles along streets, covered in mud. Radar estimates indicate that between 2.5 and 3 inches of rain occurred over the area in under an hour, resulting in the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity along the sea breeze fronts in the afternoon and early evening, with boundary mergers across interior counties of the Suwannee River Valley in the evening. Unstable mid levels with lapse rates around 6-7 degC/km but weak shear promoted gusty winds as the main hazards in strong to severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water entered a restaurant in Homerville. The water was ankle deep. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated for inclusion in Storm Data.
